{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 20qq]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 20qq]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}
{{coord|26|13|39.07|N|127|46|04.68|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=title}}

During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.

Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|12=draft}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王|12=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏|12=draft}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=draft}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=draft}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=draft}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王|12=draft}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=draft}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀|12=draft}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=draft}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁|12=draft}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=draft}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王|12=draft}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=draft}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=draft}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}
{{coord|26|13|39.07|N|127|46|04.68|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=title}}

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|内間御殿を構成する東江御殿]] '''内間御殿'''（うちまうどぅん）は、[[沖縄県]][[中頭郡]][[西原町]]にある祭祀場。[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[2月7日]]、国の[[史跡]]に指定された。
==概要== 内間御殿は、[[第二尚氏]]王統の始祖である金丸（後の[[尚円王]]）が、内間地頭に任ぜられた際の旧宅跡地に、尚円王没後190年を経た[[1666年]]に創建された神殿およびその祭祀空間であり<ref name="report">西原町教育委員会 2012『内間御殿 国指定シンポジウム報告書』 p.3~5</ref><ref name="choshi4">西原町史編算委員会 1989『西原町史 第四巻 資料編三 西原の民俗』西原町役場 p.260~261</ref>、北側にある西江御殿と南側にある東江御殿から構成される<ref name="choshi5">西原町史編纂委員会 1999『西原町史 第五巻 資料編四 西原の考古』西原町役場 p.169~170</ref>。
金丸は、越来王子（後の[[尚泰久王]]）の家臣となり、[[1454年]]に内間地頭に任ぜられた<ref name="report" />。金丸は内間地頭時代に嘉手苅の真むた親部の妹を御手掛（[[側室]]）に迎え入れた<ref name="choshi4"></ref>。そのためか、金丸が住んでいた旧宅地は大殿内と呼ばれるようになる<ref name="report" />。
==所在地== * 沖縄県中頭郡西原町字嘉手苅上之松42の1、50、51番地
==歴史== ===成立=== 内間御殿は、尚円王の没後190年に当たる1666年頃に、当時[[摂政]]であった[[羽地朝秀]]（向象賢）の進言によって、大殿内の屋敷に[[切妻造]]の茅葺（2間×3間）の神殿（東江御殿）として建立された<ref name="report" />。そのとき、御神体として[[青磁]]の枕が安置された<ref name="choshi5" />。当時、東江御殿は竹垣で周辺を囲まれており、まだ石垣積みで囲まれてはいなかった<ref name="choshi4" />。
===王府による聖地化=== [[1689年]]には、琉球王府の祭祀を司る役所である[[大美御殿]]が破損した東江御殿を、樫木を用いた瓦葺の神殿へと改築する<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。これにより、内間御殿は王府に関わる[[聖地]]としての価値が見出され、王府により整備されていった<ref name="report" />。
[[1706年]]、西原[[間切]]の住民によって、内間御殿の北側に茅葺の神殿（3間×2間半）が普請され、「西江御殿」と呼ばれるようになった。
===宝枕盗難事件と石垣の整備=== [[1735年]]に宝枕が盗まれる事件が発生し、これを機に翌[[1736年]]、本殿の屋敷囲いは竹垣から石垣積みに変わり、本門と小門を設け、門扉には[[閂]]が取り付けられ、神殿も瓦葺に変えられた<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />。
[[1737年]]には、西江御殿も瓦葺に改築され、屋敷の周囲には竹垣が張り巡らされるようになった<ref name="report" />。
[[1738年]]には、[[尚敬王]]が直筆で書いたとされる「致和」の扁額が東江御殿の本門の軒に掲げられた<ref name="bunkazai">西原町教育委員会 2013 『西原町の文化財』 p.6~7</ref>。また、尚敬王が御殿が改修までに到った経緯を刻んだ『先王旧宅碑』が内庭に建てられた<ref name="choshi4" />。
===沖縄戦と復興=== [[太平洋戦争]]末期の[[1945年]]、両御殿は[[沖縄戦]]に巻き込まれて焼失し、『先王旧宅碑』も砲弾等により一部を残し損壊した<ref name="report" />。
[[1951年]]には、大屋[[門中]]（大殿内一族）や[[ハワイ]]へ移住した一門の寄付により、東江御殿跡はトタン葺の神屋（神殿）に再建された<ref name="report" />。西江御殿は、西江御殿の御殿守であった伊礼（イリー）[[門中]]により、戦後に茅葺の神屋（神殿）へと再建された<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
[[1960年]]、東江御殿は木造の[[トタン]]葺（2間×2間）の神屋に再び改修され<ref name="report" />、[[1974年]]には、コンクリートブロック造りの現在の神屋（2間×2間半）に改築されている。改築を行ったのは、大屋門中の当主である中山正雄である<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
==宝枕== 1735年、本殿である東江御殿に盗賊が入り、御神体の宝枕が盗まれるという事件があった。宝枕の捜索には、尚敬王が自ら家臣を引き連れ、田の中から発見したという<ref name="bunkazai" />。
しかし、[[1842年]]に再び東江御殿は盗賊に入られ、安置されていた宝枕もまた盗まれ、そのまま行方不明となった<ref name="report" />。そのため、[[1835年]][[師走]]、西江御殿に安置されていた青磁の小皿を改めて東江御殿の御神体とした<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[九州・沖縄地方の史跡一覧]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html 【文化財】内間御殿] - 西原町 * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html 【文化財】先王旧宅碑] - 西原町 * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|00003707|内間御殿}}
{{coord|26|13|39.07|N|127|46|04.68|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=title}}
{{デフォルトソート:うちまうとうん}} [[Category:御嶽 (沖縄)]] [[Category:沖縄県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:第二尚氏]] [[Category:西原町]]

[[ファイル:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|内間御殿を構成する東江御殿]] '''内間御殿'''（うちまうどぅん）は、[[沖縄県]][[中頭郡]][[西原町]]にある祭祀場。[[2011年]]（平成23年）[[2月7日]]、国の[[史跡]]に指定された。
==概要== 内間御殿は、[[第二尚氏]]王統の始祖である金丸（後の[[尚円王]]）が、内間地頭に任ぜられた際の旧宅跡地に、尚円王没後190年を経た[[1666年]]に創建された神殿およびその祭祀空間であり<ref name="report">西原町教育委員会 2012『内間御殿 国指定シンポジウム報告書』 p.3~5</ref><ref name="choshi4">西原町史編算委員会 1989『西原町史 第四巻 資料編三 西原の民俗』西原町役場 p.260~261</ref>、北側にある西江御殿と南側にある東江御殿から構成される<ref name="choshi5">西原町史編纂委員会 1999『西原町史 第五巻 資料編四 西原の考古』西原町役場 p.169~170</ref>。
金丸は、越来王子（後の[[尚泰久王]]）の家臣となり、[[1454年]]に内間地頭に任ぜられた<ref name="report" />。金丸は内間地頭時代に嘉手苅の真むた親部の妹を御手掛（[[側室]]）に迎え入れた<ref name="choshi4"></ref>。そのためか、金丸が住んでいた旧宅地は大殿内と呼ばれるようになる<ref name="report" />。
==所在地== * 沖縄県中頭郡西原町字嘉手苅上之松42の1、50、51番地
==歴史== ===成立=== 内間御殿は、尚円王の没後190年に当たる1666年頃に、当時[[摂政]]であった[[羽地朝秀]]（向象賢）の進言によって、大殿内の屋敷に[[切妻造]]の茅葺（2間×3間）の神殿（東江御殿）として建立された<ref name="report" />。そのとき、御神体として[[青磁]]の枕が安置された<ref name="choshi5" />。当時、東江御殿は竹垣で周辺を囲まれており、まだ石垣積みで囲まれてはいなかった<ref name="choshi4" />。
===王府による聖地化=== [[1689年]]には、琉球王府の祭祀を司る役所である[[大美御殿]]が破損した東江御殿を、樫木を用いた瓦葺の神殿へと改築する<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。これにより、内間御殿は王府に関わる[[聖地]]としての価値が見出され、王府により整備されていった<ref name="report" />。
[[1706年]]、西原[[間切]]の住民によって、内間御殿の北側に茅葺の神殿（3間×2間半）が普請され、「西江御殿」と呼ばれるようになった。
===宝枕盗難事件と石垣の整備=== [[1735年]]に宝枕が盗まれる事件が発生し、これを機に翌[[1736年]]、本殿の屋敷囲いは竹垣から石垣積みに変わり、本門と小門を設け、門扉には[[閂]]が取り付けられ、神殿も瓦葺に変えられた<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />。
[[1737年]]には、西江御殿も瓦葺に改築され、屋敷の周囲には竹垣が張り巡らされるようになった<ref name="report" />。
[[1738年]]には、[[尚敬王]]が直筆で書いたとされる「致和」の扁額が東江御殿の本門の軒に掲げられた<ref name="bunkazai">西原町教育委員会 2013 『西原町の文化財』 p.6~7</ref>。また、尚敬王が御殿が改修までに到った経緯を刻んだ『先王旧宅碑』が内庭に建てられた<ref name="choshi4" />。
===沖縄戦と復興=== [[太平洋戦争]]末期の[[1945年]]、両御殿は[[沖縄戦]]に巻き込まれて焼失し、『先王旧宅碑』も砲弾等により一部を残し損壊した<ref name="report" />。
[[1951年]]には、大屋[[門中]]（大殿内一族）や[[ハワイ]]へ移住した一門の寄付により、東江御殿跡はトタン葺の神屋（神殿）に再建された<ref name="report" />。西江御殿は、西江御殿の御殿守であった伊礼（イリー）[[門中]]により、戦後に茅葺の神屋（神殿）へと再建された<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
[[1960年]]、東江御殿は木造の[[トタン]]葺（2間×2間）の神屋に再び改修され<ref name="report" />、[[1974年]]には、コンクリートブロック造りの現在の神屋（2間×2間半）に改築されている。改築を行ったのは、大屋門中の当主である中山正雄である<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
==宝枕== 1735年、本殿である東江御殿に盗賊が入り、御神体の宝枕が盗まれるという事件があった。宝枕の捜索には、尚敬王が自ら家臣を引き連れ、田の中から発見したという<ref name="bunkazai" />。
しかし、[[1842年]]に再び東江御殿は盗賊に入られ、安置されていた宝枕もまた盗まれ、そのまま行方不明となった<ref name="report" />。そのため、[[1835年]][[師走]]、西江御殿に安置されていた青磁の小皿を改めて東江御殿の御神体とした<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[九州・沖縄地方の史跡一覧]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html 【文化財】内間御殿] - 西原町 * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html 【文化財】先王旧宅碑] - 西原町 * {{国指定文化財等データベース|401|00003707|内間御殿}}

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}
{{coord|26|13|39.07|N|127|46|04.68|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=title}} {{Okinawan Shrines}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:A}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Uchima Udun}} {{translated page|ja|内間御殿|version=103273028|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Utaki (Okinawa)]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Second Shō Dynasty]] [[Category:Nishihara, Okinawa]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }}
[[ja:内間御殿]] [[sn:Uchima Udun]] [[sn:chima Udun]]

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}

"Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|ja|西原町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|ja|中頭郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|ja|沖縄県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in [[1666]], 190 years after the death of {{ill|King Shō En|ja|尚円王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō Dynasty|ja|第二尚氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|King Shō Taikyū|ja|尚泰久王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in [[1454]]<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Haneji Chōshū|ja|羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|celadon|ja|青磁|qq=draft|qq=draft}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In [[1689]], the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In [[1706]], the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In [[1735]], the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ([[1736]]), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring [[bolt (fastener)|bolt locks]]. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In [[1737]], Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In [[1738]], King {{ill|Shō Kei|ja|尚敬王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of [[World War II]] in [[1945]], both shrines were destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]], and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In [[1951]], donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to [[Hawaii]] led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In [[1960]], Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In [[1974]], it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in [[1842]], another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in [[1835]], a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}
{{coord|26|13|39.07|N|127|46|04.68|E|type:landmark_region:JP-47|display=title}} {{Okinawan Shrines}}
{{translated page|ja|内間御殿|version=103273028|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Utaki (Okinawa)]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Second Shō Dynasty]] [[Category:Nishihara, Okinawa]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}
