revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q56346970]] Wikidata name: Uchima Udun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|en|Nishihara, Okinawa|WD=Q1206796|lt=Nishihara, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|en|Nakagami District, Okinawa|WD=Q1205862|lt=Nakagami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in {{ill|1666|en|1666|WD=Q7585|lt=1666}}, 190 years after the death of {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=King Shō En}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō dynasty|en|Second Shō dynasty|WD=Q11602290|lt=Second Shō Dynasty}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agari Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Gushichan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|WD=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in {{ill|1454|en|1454|WD=Q6476|lt=1454}}<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdonuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|WD=Q843010|lt=Haneji Chōshū}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=rake-overhanging gabled roof|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13308575}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|en|Nishihara, Okinawa|WD=Q1206796|lt=Nishihara, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|en|Nakagami District, Okinawa|WD=Q1205862|lt=Nakagami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in {{ill|1666|en|1666|WD=Q7585|lt=1666}}, 190 years after the death of {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=King Shō En}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō dynasty|en|Second Shō dynasty|WD=Q11602290|lt=Second Shō Dynasty}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agari Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Gushichan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|WD=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in {{ill|1454|en|1454|WD=Q6476|lt=1454}}<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdonuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|WD=Q843010|lt=Haneji Chōshū}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=rake-overhanging gabled roof|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13308575}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|en|Nishihara, Okinawa|WD=Q1206796|lt=Nishihara, Okinawa|1=Nishihara, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|en|Nakagami District, Okinawa|WD=Q1205862|lt=Nakagami District, Okinawa|1=Nakagami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in {{ill|1666|en|1666|WD=Q7585|lt=1666|1=1666}}, 190 years after the death of {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=King Shō En|1=Shō En}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō dynasty|en|Second Shō dynasty|WD=Q11602290|lt=Second Shō Dynasty|1=Second Shō dynasty}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agari Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Gushichan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|WD=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū|1=Shō Taikyū}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in {{ill|1454|en|1454|WD=Q6476|lt=1454|1=1454}}<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdonuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|WD=Q843010|lt=Haneji Chōshū|1=Shō Shōken}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13308575}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon|1=Celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689|1=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706|1=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735|1=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736|1=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks|1=Bolt (fastener)}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737|1=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738|1=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei|1=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951|1=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960|1=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974|1=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842|1=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835|1=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[List of Historic Sites of Kyushu and Okinawa]]
== External links == {{Commonscat|Uchima Udun}} * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/27uchimagoden.html Uchima Udun - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * [http://www.town.nishihara.okinawa.jp/asset/15senohkyutakuhi.html Former Residence Monument of the Late King - Cultural Property of Nishihara Town] * {{Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties|401|00003707|Uchima Udun}}

In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706|1=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."

In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736|1=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks|1=Bolt (fastener)}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737|1=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738|1=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei|1=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951|1=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960|1=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974|1=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842|1=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835|1=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Uchima Udun]][[en:Uchimaudūn]][[ja:内間御殿]]
{{infobox religious building |name=Uchima Udun |image=File:Agari Udun, Uchima Udun at Nishihara 202410.jpg|thumb|caption=Agari Udun, part of Uchima Udun }}
'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|en|Nishihara, Okinawa|WD=Q1206796|lt=Nishihara, Okinawa|1=Nishihara, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|en|Nakagami District, Okinawa|WD=Q1205862|lt=Nakagami District, Okinawa|1=Nakagami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in {{ill|1666|en|1666|WD=Q7585|lt=1666|1=1666}}, 190 years after the death of {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=King Shō En|1=Shō En}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō dynasty|en|Second Shō dynasty|WD=Q11602290|lt=Second Shō Dynasty|1=Second Shō dynasty}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agari Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Gushichan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|WD=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū|1=Shō Taikyū}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in {{ill|1454|en|1454|WD=Q6476|lt=1454|1=1454}}<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdonuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|WD=Q843010|lt=Haneji Chōshū|1=Shō Shōken}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13308575}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon|1=Celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689|1=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706|1=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735|1=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736|1=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks|1=Bolt (fastener)}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737|1=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738|1=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei|1=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951|1=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960|1=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974|1=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842|1=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835|1=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.

'''Uchima Udun''' (内間御殿) is a sacred site in {{ill|Nishihara, Okinawa|en|Nishihara, Okinawa|WD=Q1206796|lt=Nishihara, Okinawa|1=Nishihara, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Nakagami District, Okinawa|en|Nakagami District, Okinawa|WD=Q1205862|lt=Nakagami District, Okinawa|1=Nakagami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It was designated a [[Historic Site of Japan|National Historic Site]] on [[February 7, 2011]] (Heisei 23).
== Overview == Uchima Udun was established in {{ill|1666|en|1666|WD=Q7585|lt=1666|1=1666}}, 190 years after the death of {{ill|Shō En|en|Shō En|WD=Q1074326|lt=King Shō En|1=Shō En}}, the founder of the {{ill|Second Shō dynasty|en|Second Shō dynasty|WD=Q11602290|lt=Second Shō Dynasty|1=Second Shō dynasty}}. It was built on the former residence of Kanamaru (later King Shō En) when he was appointed chieftain of Uchima<ref name="report">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2012). "Uchima Udun: National Designation Symposium Report," pp. 3-5</ref><ref name="choshi4">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1989). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 4: Folklore of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 260-261</ref>. Uchima Udun consists of the {{ill|Seie Udun|ja|西江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seie Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the north and the {{ill|Agari Udun|ja|東江御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agari Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to the south<ref name="choshi5">Nishihara Town History Compilation Committee (1999). "Nishihara Town History, Volume 5: Archaeological Materials of Nishihara," Nishihara Town Office, pp. 169-170</ref>.
Kanamaru was a vassal of {{ill|Prince Gushichan|ja|越来王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Gushichan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Shō Taikyū|en|Shō Taikyū|WD=Q1139919|lt=King Shō Taikyū|1=Shō Taikyū}}) and was appointed chieftain of Uchima in {{ill|1454|en|1454|WD=Q6476|lt=1454|1=1454}}<ref name="report" />. During this time, he took the younger sister of Kamuta Shinmuta of Kadekaru as a concubine<ref name="choshi4"></ref>. As a result, the former residence of Kanamaru came to be called {{ill|Ōdonuchi|ja|大殿内|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōdonuchi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}<ref name="report" />.
== Location == * Uchima Udun is located at 42-1, 50, and 51 Uenomatsu, Kadekaru, Nishihara Town, Nakagami District, Okinawa Prefecture.
== History == === Establishment === In 1666, on the advice of the then-[[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|WD=Q843010|lt=Haneji Chōshū|1=Shō Shōken}} (Shōken), a thatched {{ill|Kirizuma-zukuri|ja|切妻造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kirizuma-zukuri|1=Kirizuma-style|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13308575}} shrine (2 ken × 3 ken) called Agari Udun was built at the site of the Ōdonuchi residence<ref name="report" />. At this time, a {{ill|Celadon|en|Celadon|WD=Q1054559|lt=celadon|1=Celadon}} pillow was enshrined as the sacred object<ref name="choshi5" />. Originally, Agari Udun was surrounded by bamboo fencing rather than stone walls<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== Designation as a Sacred Site by the Royal Government === In {{ill|1689|en|1689|WD=Q7674|lt=1689|1=1689}}, the {{ill|Ōmii Udun|ja|大美御殿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmii Udun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the government office overseeing Ryukyuan royal rituals, reconstructed the damaged Agari Udun using oak wood and converted it into a tiled shrine<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />. This officially recognized Uchima Udun as a sacred site connected to the Ryukyu Kingdom, leading to further government maintenance<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1706|en|1706|WD=Q6835|lt=1706|1=1706}}, the residents of Nishihara Magiri built a thatched shrine (3 ken × 2.5 ken) north of Uchima Udun, later known as "Seie Udun."
=== Theft of the Treasure Pillow and Fortification === In {{ill|1735|en|1735|WD=Q6984|lt=1735|1=1735}}, the treasured sacred pillow was stolen. In response, the following year ({{ill|1736|en|1736|WD=Q6989|lt=1736|1=1736}}), the bamboo fencing was replaced with stone walls, and main and side gates were installed, featuring {{ill|bolt (fastener)|en|bolt (fastener)|WD=Q125183448|lt=bolt locks|1=Bolt (fastener)}}. The shrine was also tiled<ref name="choshi4" /><ref name="choshi5" />.
In {{ill|1737|en|1737|WD=Q6993|lt=1737|1=1737}}, Seie Udun was also converted into a tiled structure, with bamboo fencing added around its perimeter<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738|1=1738}}, King {{ill|Shō Kei|en|Shō Kei|WD=Q1041401|lt=Shō Kei|1=Shō Kei}} inscribed the plaque "Chiwa" (致和), which was placed above the main gate of Agari Udun<ref name="bunkazai">Nishihara Town Board of Education (2013). "Cultural Properties of Nishihara Town," pp. 6-7</ref>. A stone monument called "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" (先王旧宅碑), detailing the history of the renovation, was erected in the inner courtyard<ref name="choshi4" />.
=== The Battle of Okinawa and Restoration === During the final stages of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} in {{ill|1945|en|1945|WD=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}}, both shrines were destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, and the "Monument of the Former Residence of the Late King" was partially damaged by shellfire<ref name="report" />.
In {{ill|1951|en|1951|WD=Q18591|lt=1951|1=1951}}, donations from the {{ill|Ōya Monchu|ja|大屋門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (descendants of the Ōdonuchi family) and Ryukyuan emigrants to {{ill|Hawaii|en|Hawaii|WD=Q782|lt=Hawaii|1=Hawaii}} led to the reconstruction of Agari Udun with a corrugated iron-roofed shrine<ref name="report" />. Seie Udun was reconstructed in thatched form by the {{ill|Irei Monchu|ja|伊礼門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irei Monchu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the original caretakers of the site<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
In {{ill|1960|en|1960|WD=Q3754|lt=1960|1=1960}}, Agari Udun was renovated into a wooden structure with a corrugated iron roof (2 ken × 2 ken)<ref name="report" />. In {{ill|1974|en|1974|WD=Q2478|lt=1974|1=1974}}, it was rebuilt in its current form using concrete blocks (2 ken × 2.5 ken) by Masao Nakayama, the head of the Ōya Monchu<ref name="bunkazai" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
== The Sacred Pillow == In 1735, thieves broke into Agari Udun and stole the treasured sacred pillow. King Shō Kei personally led his retainers in searching for the item, ultimately recovering it from a rice field<ref name="bunkazai" />.
However, in {{ill|1842|en|1842|WD=Q7625|lt=1842|1=1842}}, another burglary occurred, and the sacred pillow was stolen once again, never to be recovered<ref name="report" />. As a result, in {{ill|1835|en|1835|WD=Q7597|lt=1835|1=1835}}, a celadon dish enshrined in Seie Udun was designated as the new sacred object of Agari Udun<ref name="report" /><ref name="choshi4" />.
